food_subgroup: Donburi rice bowl with fish, egg, and seaweed

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn entry for mackerel donburi exists; closest parent 'donburi food product' (FOODON_03315595) used as the ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to mackerel donburi; A0CKP (fish-based composite dish) used as closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to mackerel donburi as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Mackerel Don is approximately 380 g, comprising a bowl of vinegared sushi rice topped with grilled or simmered mackerel, tamagoyaki, and wakame seaweed, as typically served at Japanese casual dining restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries: Atlantic Mackerel (raw, FDC ID 175097), Sushi Rice (FDC ID 169753), Tamagoyaki / Egg omelette (FDC ID 173424), and Wakame seaweed (FDC ID 168457); weighted by estimated component proportions (mackerel 35%, sushi rice 45%, tamagoyaki 12%, wakame 8%).
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry for Mackerel Don as a composite dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by weighted component blending using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods entries for each ingredient. Component weight proportions: mackerel fillet 35%, vinegared sushi rice 45%, tamagoyaki 12%, wakame 8%. All component entries are from USDA FoodData Central, an approved secondary global source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sushi rice and composite Japanese rice-based meals; analogous estimate anchored to white sushi rice GI (GI ~54) from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care).
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mackerel Don as a composite dish. The GI is estimated using vinegared sushi rice as the primary glycemic driver (GI ~54, low, per University of Sydney GI Database). Mackerel, tamagoyaki, and wakame contribute negligible glycemic load. The fat and protein content of mackerel and egg moderately attenuate the overall glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 70.3) / 100 ≈ 37.8. Carbohydrate per serving = 18.5 g/100g × 380 g serving = 70.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g; ~1178 mg per serving) from soy-seasoned sushi rice, soy-glazed mackerel, and wakame
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Mackerel Don to occasional consumption and request reduced-sodium soy seasoning, as a standard serving may contribute approximately 50% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~54) driven by vinegared sushi rice; moderate glycemic load (~37.8 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Mackerel Don in controlled portions, as the vinegared sushi rice provides a low GI but a high glycemic load per standard bowl; the omega-3-rich mackerel and protein from tamagoyaki partially attenuate postprandial glucose response but do not eliminate the need for portion control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High omega-3 fatty acid content from mackerel (EPA and DHA); moderate saturated fat (~1.1 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mackerel Don is broadly cardioprotective due to the high EPA and DHA content of mackerel, which supports triglyceride reduction and anti-inflammatory pathways; however, sodium content should be monitored in individuals with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mackerel Don (サバ丼, Saba Don) is a Japanese donburi rice bowl dish consisting of vinegared sushi rice topped with grilled or simmered mackerel (saba), tamagoyaki (Japanese rolled egg omelette), and wakame seaweed. The dish is a popular item at Japanese casual dining and conveyor-belt sushi restaurants in Singapore, including chains such as Sushi Express. The mackerel provides a rich, oily, and savoury flavour profile with a firm, flaky texture, complemented by the mild sweetness of tamagoyaki and the umami-briny character of wakame. The vinegared sushi rice adds a subtle tang and acts as the primary carbohydrate base. Nutritionally, Mackerel Don is a notable source of omega-3 long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids (EPA and DHA) from the mackerel, high-quality protein from both the fish and egg, and vitamin B12. The dish carries a low glycemic index (~54) driven by the sushi rice, and a high glycemic load per standard serving (~380 g bowl). Sodium content is moderately elevated due to soy-based seasoning in the rice and fish preparation. Clinically, the dish is broadly favourable for cardiovascular health due to its omega-3 content, but requires portion control for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. It is not suitable for those with fish, egg, soy, or gluten allergies.
common_user_queries: Is Mackerel Don good for diabetics?
Is Saba Don high in omega-3?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry for Mackerel Don as a composite dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component blending of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for mackerel, sushi rice, tamagoyaki, and wakame. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sushi rice as the primary glycemic driver. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries; no single composite entry for Mackerel Don exists in any approved primary source. Serving size of 380 g is an estimate based on typical Japanese donburi bowl portions at Singapore casual dining restaurants. trans_fat_g is 0.0; this is confirmed negligible/trace for the component ingredients per USDA FoodData Central and is not a formulated zero. Sodium is elevated (~310 mg/100 g) primarily from soy-based seasoning used in sushi rice preparation and mackerel glazing; actual restaurant sodium may vary. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value estimated from USDA component data). All per_100g values are numeric; no -1 sentinel values are used in this record as all nutrients were estimable from USDA component data. GI set to 54 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database sushi rice analogue; glycemic_load_per_serving = 37.8 calculated from 18.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 380 g serving / 100 × GI 54 / 100. FoodOn match is closest_parent (donburi food product); no exact FoodOn code for mackerel donburi exists. FoodEx2 match is closest_parent (A0CKP, fish-based composite dish); no exact FoodEx2 code for mackerel donburi exists. SNOMED CT match is none; no suitable SNOMED CT concept maps to this specific food. Halal status is unconfirmed as restaurant-level halal certification for Sushi Express Singapore is not verified in this record.
